name: Bhartrprapanca
dates_notes: Known only via Śaṅkara's polemic in the Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya; no surviving work; reconstruction by Hiriyanna 1924 from citation-fragments.

lineage_polemical.note: Śaṅkara's Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya is the principal source for and refutation of Bhartṛprapañca's bhedābheda.

core_thesis: No work of Bhartṛprapañca survives. He is reconstructed entirely through citation-fragments preserved in his opponents — Śaṅkara's *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya*, Sureśvara's *Vārttika*, and Ānandagiri's sub-gloss — and assembled by Hiriyanna in 1924. What can be attributed to him with reasonable confidence is a *bhedābheda* reading of the *Bṛhadāraṇyaka*. On this reading, *brahman* is intrinsically both one and many: the Upaniṣadic *neti neti* (negation: "not this, not this") and the cosmogonic affirmations of Yājñavalkya are both literal, neither concessive. *Brahman* genuinely transforms (*pariṇāma*) into the manifold of *nāma-rūpa* (name-and-form) while remaining the unitary substrate. The *jīva* is a real *aṃśa* (part-portion) of *brahman*. The path to liberation requires *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*: scriptural cognition combined with continuing ritual action. *Mukti* is therefore the realization of one's identity as a genuine differentiated mode of *brahman*, not the cancellation of phenomenal plurality. Four points appear stably across the citation-fragments: the dual-aspect ontology, *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, the eight-aspect (*aṣṭa-rūpa*) account of *brahman*, and the *jīva*'s real partial identity with *brahman*. Beyond these, the reconstruction is conjectural; what survives is filtered through hostile witnesses.

engaged_works.title: Reconstructed Brhadaranyaka commentary fragments
engaged_works.ascription_notes: No work survives; the corpus is a modern reconstruction (Hiriyanna 1924) from citations preserved in Śaṅkara, Sureśvara, and Ānandagiri.
engaged_works.summary: Not a work but a reconstruction. No commentary by Bhartṛprapañca survives in any manuscript. The corpus, such as it is, was assembled by M. Hiriyanna in his 1924 article "Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin" (*The Indian Antiquary* 53), and supplemented by later studies. The fragments come from four kinds of source. First, Śaṅkara's *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya*, where Bhartṛprapañca is the principal *pūrvapakṣin* across many chapters and is named explicitly. Second, Sureśvara's *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika*, which expands Śaṅkara's refutations and preserves further passages. Third, Ānandagiri's sub-commentary, which occasionally labels unattributed positions as Bhartṛprapañca's. Fourth, scattered references in later Vedāntic literature. The reconstruction yields a *Bṛhadāraṇyaka* commentary that took the Upaniṣad to teach *bhedābheda* together with *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*. Such a reading is plausible on internal grounds: the *Bṛhadāraṇyaka* combines cosmogonic affirmation in Yājñavalkya's enumerations with the apophatic *neti neti*. The text-historical situation, however, remains irreducibly speculative. Every fragment passes through a hostile filter, and what is called "Bhartṛprapañca's doctrine" is in the first instance a Śaṅkaran summary, not an authorial voice.

key_passages.locus_long: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 1.4.7
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tad dha idaṃ tarhy avyākṛtam āsīt ।
tan nāmarūpābhyām eva vyākriyata ॥
key_passages.text_critical_note: Reconstruction-only entry: the thinker's own prose is lost; Hiriyanna's article anchors the doctrine at this Bṛhadāraṇyaka locus, and the Sanskrit is normalized from local Vedānta witnesses rather than taken from authorial autograph.

samasa_vigrahas.note: privative compound for the unmanifest state
the pair through which manifestation becomes determinate

verb_modality.note: middle/passive value in context: manifestation becomes articulated

key_passages.english_close: Then this was unmanifest. It was differentiated precisely by name and form.
key_passages.why_this_passage: Hiriyanna's reconstruction makes Bhartṛprapañca's realism about manifestation turn on exactly this transition from the unmanifest to articulated nāma-rūpa. It is a fair anchor because the surviving evidence repeatedly presents him as taking such cosmological passages literally, not as merely provisional concessions.
key_passages.source_edition: M. Hiriyanna, 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin,' The Indian Antiquary 53 (1924), reconstruction article; locus normalized against local Vedānta citation witnesses.

key_passages.locus_long: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.3.1 and 2.3.6
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
dve vāva brahmaṇo rūpe mūrtaṃ caivāmūrtaṃ ca ।
neti neti ॥
key_passages.text_critical_note: This is a reconstructed lemma-cluster rather than continuous surviving commentary. Hiriyanna uses these loci to show that Bhartṛprapañca reads the positive two-form teaching and the apophatic 'neti neti' together, not competitively.
key_passages.english_close: Brahman has, indeed, two forms: the formed and the formless. 'Not this, not this.'

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: 'not this, not this,' the apophatic denial of exhaustive predication

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the clearest on-disk anchor for Hiriyanna's claim that Bhartṛprapañca does not flatten the Upaniṣad into either sheer plurality or sheer negation. It fairly represents him because his distinctiveness lies in insisting that the positive dual-aspect teaching and the apophatic limit-statement are both philosophically operative.
key_passages.source_edition: M. Hiriyanna, 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin,' The Indian Antiquary 53 (1924), reconstruction article; loci normalized against local Vedānta citation witnesses.

key_passages.locus_long: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 1.4.10
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
ahaṃ brahmāsmi ।
atha yo 'nyāṃ devatām upāste 'nyo 'sāv anyo 'ham asmīti na sa veda ॥
key_passages.text_critical_note: Reconstruction-only entry: Hiriyanna treats Bhartṛprapañca as fully accepting the abheda-vākyas, but not in a way that cancels the real manifold elsewhere. The passage is therefore selected as a doctrinal anchor, not as proof of later Advaitic reduction.
key_passages.english_close: I am Brahman. But he who worships another deity, thinking 'He is one thing and I am another,' does not know.
key_passages.why_this_passage: Hiriyanna's reconstruction is explicit that Bhartṛprapañca accepts the identity-statements and is not simply a pluralist counter-voice to them. This passage is fair because it shows the anti-separative force he must accommodate, while his distinct doctrine consists in refusing to treat that force as requiring the unreality of manifestation.
key_passages.source_edition: M. Hiriyanna, 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin,' The Indian Antiquary 53 (1924), reconstruction article; locus normalized against local Vedānta citation witnesses.

key_passages.locus_long: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.4.5 and 1.4.15
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
ātmā vā are draṣṭavyaḥ śrotavyo mantavyo nididhyāsitavyaḥ ।
ātmānam eva lokam upāsīta ॥
key_passages.text_critical_note: This is a reconstruction-cluster: no continuous Bhartṛprapañca autograph survives, and Hiriyanna's article uses precisely such Bṛhadāraṇyaka loci to explain why his soteriology cannot be reduced to instant sentence-cognition alone.

karaka_structure.note: predicate accusative: the self as the world

key_passages.english_close: The self, dear one, is to be seen, to be heard about, to be reflected upon, and to be meditated upon. One should worship the self alone as the world.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the fairest on-disk anchor for the reconstructed claim that Bhartṛprapañca does not make liberation hinge on a single bare identity-sentence heard once. Hiriyanna's reconstruction repeatedly turns on the continuing force of hearing, reflection, meditation, and upāsanā; the stronger jñāna-karma-samuccaya thesis is preserved only indirectly in the later polemical record.
key_passages.source_edition: M. Hiriyanna, 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin,' The Indian Antiquary 53 (1924), reconstruction article; loci normalized against local Vedānta citation witnesses.
